title: SEGMENT 13 — First dose — the escalating intrathecal regimen

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — First dose — the escalating intrathecal regimen
In late January of 2018, Mila Makovec received the first intrathecal injection of milasen.

Roughly twelve months had elapsed between the referral of her case to Yu's lab and the moment a needle, loaded with a drug that had been designed and manufactured for her alone, was placed into her lower back and the drug was infused into her cerebrospinal fluid.
Twelve months.
That is, to put it gently, an unprecedented turnaround for a brand-new drug.
The clinical regimen used what is called a dose escalation strategy.
Rather than going straight to the eventual maintenance dose, the team started low and ramped up over a series of doses, watching carefully at each step for adverse effects.
The reason for this caution is that even with all the platform inheritance from nusinersen, this was the first time this specific sequence had ever been administered to a human being.
If something unexpected was going to happen, the safest place to discover it was at a low dose where any adverse effect would, hopefully, be milder and more easily managed.
The escalation went, in milligrams: three point five, then seven, then fourteen, then twenty-one, then twenty-eight, then thirty-five, then forty-two.
Seven loading doses, each administered roughly two weeks apart over the first several months of treatment.
Then two additional doses at the full forty-two milligram level, also at two-week intervals, to complete the loading phase.
After that, the regimen transitioned to maintenance dosing: forty-two milligrams approximately every three months, the same kind of long-interval maintenance schedule used for nusinersen.
The forty-two milligram dose, as I mentioned in Segment 11, was chosen on the basis of two considerations.
First, intrathecally administered ASOs do not distribute uniformly throughout the central nervous system.

They tend to reach higher concentrations in the spinal cord and the lower CSF spaces, and lower concentrations in the brain itself, because the CSF flow patterns and the diffusion characteristics of the drug favor caudal — that is, toward-the-spine — distribution.
Estimates from preclinical and pharmacokinetic studies suggested that the brain reaches roughly one-third the exposure that the spinal cord does.

Since Mila's disease is primarily a brain disease — her affected neurons are in the cerebrum and cerebellum — the team needed a dose high enough that even after the discount for poor brain distribution, the drug would reach meaningful concentrations in her brain tissue.
Three times the nusinersen dose, in molar terms, was the answer that fell out of that math.
Second, the dose had to be below whatever level might cause acute toxicity.
The rat study had shown transient hindlimb weakness at doses corresponding to the upper end of the human nusinersen range, but no permanent damage.
The forty-two milligram dose was selected to deliver a meaningful brain exposure while staying below thresholds that might cause acute adverse effects.
In total, across the loading phase and the first year of maintenance dosing reported in the paper, approximately eighteen grams of milasen drug substance were manufactured.
Eighteen grams.
For comparison, a typical small-molecule drug program might produce tens of kilograms or more across the development and commercial phases combined.
Milasen is, almost certainly, the smallest production run for any individually-manufactured pharmaceutical in modern history.
There was no commercial manufacturing process.
The drug was synthesized to research-grade standards under a custom protocol, with quality controls appropriate for its intended use in a single patient.
How was the dose administered, in practice?
Mila came to the hospital.
She was sedated, because intrathecal injection in a young child with active neurological disease is not a procedure you do on an awake, frightened patient.
Under sedation, a needle was placed in her lower back, between two of her lower lumbar vertebrae, into the subarachnoid space where the cerebrospinal fluid pools.

A small volume of CSF was withdrawn first — both to confirm correct needle placement and to provide a sample for laboratory analysis — and then the dose of milasen, dissolved in a small volume of saline, was slowly infused.
The whole procedure took perhaps fifteen to twenty minutes from start to finish.
Mila recovered from sedation, was observed in the hospital for several hours to confirm no acute adverse effects, and then went home.
This was repeated approximately every two weeks during loading, and every three months during maintenance.
The CSF samples collected at each dosing visit allowed the team to measure the pharmacokinetics of the drug — how much milasen was present in the CSF at various time points after dosing.
The concentrations rose in approximate proportion to the dose given, plateauing in the range of about eighteen to twenty-seven nanograms per milliliter during the maintenance phase.
There was one trough — a dip in CSF drug levels at around day forty — that was attributed to a small CSF leak following one of the procedures.
Otherwise, the pharmacokinetics behaved roughly as expected from the platform precedent.

The drug was getting into the CSF, persisting there, presumably reaching the neurons, and presumably being taken up by them — though of course there was no direct way to measure tissue concentrations in the patient's brain.
Across the loading phase and the first year of maintenance dosing, there were no serious adverse events.
That is one of the most important sentences in the paper, and I want you to register it.
No serious adverse events.

The drug did not, at the doses used, cause neurological deterioration, did not cause meningitis or other infectious complications of repeated intrathecal access, did not cause systemic toxicity that was attributable to the drug, did not cause any abnormality in routine laboratory monitoring that required intervention.

There were minor adverse events of the kind you'd expect from any patient receiving multiple intrathecal procedures — some procedural pain, some transient changes in CSF cell counts, the CSF leak I mentioned — but nothing serious, nothing that required stopping the drug, nothing that suggested the platform was unsafe for this patient.
That is, in a sense, the first answer the paper gives to its central question.
Can we build a medicine for one person?
Yes, and we can deliver it without immediately harming her.
The first hurdle — that the brand-new drug, when injected into a child's spinal fluid, would not cause an acute disaster — was cleared.
Now the question turned to whether the drug, beyond being safe, was actually doing anything.
